Hey, JP here in the house for your daily motivation slash skills training. So the very sad day for me. I'm about to leave for New York for my dad's funeral. He's still alive at this moment, but he was a few hours to go. And so I'm flying out there today. I did get to see him one last time last week, which is awesome and got a lot of great memories and interesting topic here now because we're on the week of beliefs, right? The beliefs that kind of dictate our actions and allow us to make sense of the world and how we move through the world and. A lot of the beliefs that we get, we get from our parents. They start, they start right there, you know, and in some respects, you know, you see your parents and you either adopt the beliefs they have or you'll go the exact opposite way. And in some respects, I went the exact opposite way as my parents when it came to certain core beliefs about money and success and sales and marketing and wealth creation. And it's an interesting thing to look back at my dad's life and say that I watched him struggle so badly. When it came to really expressing his abilities and his talents and his greatness, because he had such talent, my dad, so smart, quick, great with numbers, you know, but he struggled badly in the workplace until, you know, I became who I became and he went to work for me because of certain beliefs he had about change and taking action and just risk, you know, reward. It all starts with the beliefs, you know, he developed certain beliefs, you know, and they would really, these group of beliefs I call depression, era mentality beliefs where, you know, where everything is based on scarcity and, you know, it's a belief that's almost rooted in. I believe that I can win in life or at least, you know, sort of, you know, how about this? Not when at least I can move through life by playing defense versus offense. You get it? I believe in playing defense. I played defensive life versus offensive life. I'll tell you something. You're never going to win at life, at the game of life. You're never going to have the life that you're truly capable of, the robust, amazing, colorful life filled with abundance and joy. You're never going to get that by playing defense. It's not always about, you know, worrying about what you might lose versus what you can win, what you can gain. And that was a belief that was so rooted in my parents, both of them, money about money and money was difficult to find. It was difficult to make money, that you had to sacrifice your integrity for money, that you had to like the people to make money, that you had to work extra hard to make money. When I watch my parents work really, really, really hard their whole lives and not make hardly any money. Seriously. I make more in one speech now in an hour than they would make combined in a year. Now, granted, there's been inflation since then, but let's just say that even without inflation, I'd say it's probably almost close. All right. Now, I mean, disrespect to my parents are amazing people. My mom is still alive and my dad now, you know, just passed, but I was about to pass today. But they would be the first ones to admit that their beliefs about money and success really held them back from living the life they could leave. Seriously. They lived in fear of what they could lose. They thought that selling was evil. They thought that, you know, rich people were always greedy and and that marketing was manipulative. I just got to tell you, if you have any of those beliefs lurking in the back of your mind that in some way that selling and marketing is evil or manipulative, then you are going to self sabotage. You get it? You're going to self sabotage your own success. Bottom line. And if it's, let's say, a belief, but not a very strong one, and you have another belief that's like, I want to make, you know, I believe I need to make money to be, to have a good life. But what happens is, yes, you still will do well, but you'll be conflicted. You'll feel, you'll feel nervous. You'll feel bad about it. You won't have that feeling of being congruent. That, you know, I feel my best when I'm making the most money. I just do. I absolutely do. I feel like I'm resonating on all cylinders. I'm firing on all cylinders. My life is in flow. It's what it should be. I don't have any limiting beliefs when it comes to making money. I know money is good. I know they'll have to sacrifice my integrity or ethics to make a lot of money. We worked together for the long term. But the notion that, you know, there's something in the back of your mind that's going to stop you from applying this at the highest level and make you feel bad about that thought just drives me crazy.
